Topics.Title: Introduction to the HTML5 Canvas Element
Topics.Summary: Students learn what the HTML5 Canvas element is, how to add it to a webpage, and how to access the 2D rendering context using JavaScript to begin drawing programmatically.

Elements.Title: What is the HTML5 Canvas Element?
Elements.BodyText: The HTML5 Canvas element is a rectangular drawing surface defined in HTML that allows developers to render graphics dynamically using JavaScript.
Elements.Notes: Unlike static image elements, the canvas itself contains no visual content by default — all drawing is done programmatically through scripting.

Items.Text: The <canvas> element acts as a blank bitmap surface embedded directly in an HTML page.
It was introduced in HTML5 to enable client-side, scriptable rendering of 2D and 3D graphics without plugins.
Common use cases include data visualizations, animations, game graphics, and interactive diagrams.

Elements.Title: Adding the Canvas Element to a Webpage
Elements.BodyText: Placing a canvas on a webpage requires a single HTML tag with optional width and height attributes to define its dimensions.
Elements.Notes: Example: <canvas id="myCanvas" width="600" height="400"></canvas>. If width and height are omitted, the canvas defaults to 300×150 pixels.

Items.Text: Use the <canvas> tag with a unique id attribute so JavaScript can reference it easily.
Set width and height in the HTML attributes rather than CSS to avoid unintended scaling of the drawing surface.
Fallback content placed between the opening and closing tags is displayed in browsers that do not support the canvas element.

Elements.Title: Selecting the Canvas Element with JavaScript
Elements.BodyText: Before any drawing can occur, JavaScript must obtain a reference to the canvas DOM element using a selection method.
Elements.Notes: The most common approach is document.getElementById('myCanvas'), though querySelector can also be used.

Items.Text: Storing the canvas reference in a variable allows repeated access without querying the DOM multiple times.
The canvas reference itself is not used for drawing — it is the gateway to obtaining the rendering context.
Scripts should run after the DOM has loaded (e.g., placed before </body> or inside a DOMContentLoaded listener) to ensure the element exists.

Elements.Title: Understanding the Rendering Context
Elements.BodyText: The rendering context is the object that exposes the drawing API; it must be retrieved from the canvas element before any graphics can be produced.
Elements.Notes: The context type string '2d' returns a CanvasRenderingContext2D object. Other context types such as 'webgl' support 3D rendering but are outside this module's scope.

Items.Text: Call the canvas element's getContext() method and pass the string '2d' to obtain a 2D rendering context.
The returned context object contains all the properties and methods used to draw shapes, text, images, and paths.
Storing the context in a variable (conventionally named ctx) is standard practice for readability and convenience.

Elements.Title: Writing the Basic Canvas Setup Code
Elements.BodyText: A minimal canvas setup involves three lines of JavaScript: selecting the element, getting the context, and verifying the result before drawing.
Elements.Notes: Example: const canvas = document.getElementById('myCanvas'); const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d'); — after these two lines, ctx is ready for drawing commands.

Items.Text: Selecting the canvas and calling getContext('2d') are always the first two steps in any canvas-based drawing script.
A simple check such as if (ctx) { ... } guards against errors in environments where canvas is unsupported.
Once the context is available, drawing commands can be chained immediately or organized into reusable functions.

Elements.Title: Canvas Coordinate System
Elements.BodyText: The canvas uses a 2D coordinate system where the origin (0, 0) is located at the top-left corner, with x increasing to the right and y increasing downward.
Elements.Notes: Understanding this coordinate system is essential because it differs from traditional mathematical graphs where y increases upward.

Items.Text: Every pixel position on the canvas is addressed as an (x, y) pair measured in pixels from the top-left origin.
The canvas width determines the maximum x value, and the height determines the maximum y value.
Coordinates outside the canvas boundaries are not rendered, so drawings must be positioned within the defined dimensions.

Topics.Title: Drawing Basic Shapes
Topics.Summary: This topic covers how to draw fundamental shapes such as rectangles, circles, and polygons using the Canvas API's built-in methods and properties.

Elements.Title: Setting Up the Canvas Context
Elements.BodyText: Before drawing any shapes, you must obtain a 2D rendering context from the canvas element using JavaScript.
Elements.Notes: Use document.getElementById('myCanvas').getContext('2d') to retrieve the context object, commonly stored in a variable named 'ctx'. All drawing methods are called on this context object.

Items.Text: The canvas element is defined in HTML with a specified width and height, e.g., <canvas id='myCanvas' width='500' height='400'></canvas>.
The getContext('2d') method returns a CanvasRenderingContext2D object that exposes all drawing APIs.
Always check that the browser supports the Canvas API before attempting to draw.

Elements.Title: Drawing Rectangles
Elements.BodyText: The Canvas API provides three built-in methods specifically for drawing rectangles, making them the simplest shapes to render.
Elements.Notes: Rectangles are the only shapes with dedicated non-path methods in the Canvas API. Parameters follow the pattern (x, y, width, height).

Items.Text: fillRect(x, y, width, height) draws a solid filled rectangle at the specified position and size.
strokeRect(x, y, width, height) draws only the outline (border) of a rectangle without filling it.
clearRect(x, y, width, height) erases a rectangular area, making it fully transparent, useful for animation or clearing the canvas.

Elements.Title: Drawing Circles and Arcs
Elements.BodyText: Circles and arcs are drawn using the arc() method as part of a path, since there is no dedicated circle method in the Canvas API.
Elements.Notes: A full circle is achieved by setting the startAngle to 0 and the endAngle to 2 * Math.PI. Partial values create arc segments.

Items.Text: The arc() method signature is arc(x, y, radius, startAngle, endAngle, anticlockwise), where x and y define the center point.
Angles are measured in radians; convert degrees to radians using the formula: radians = degrees * (Math.PI / 180).
After calling arc(), use fill() to create a filled circle or stroke() to render only the circle's outline.
The optional anticlockwise boolean parameter (default false) controls the direction in which the arc is drawn.

Elements.Title: Drawing Polygons and Lines with Paths
Elements.BodyText: Multi-sided shapes like triangles, pentagons, and custom polygons are constructed by defining a series of connected line segments using path methods.
Elements.Notes: Calling closePath() draws a straight line back to the starting point, completing the shape. Without it, the shape remains open.

Items.Text: Begin a new path with beginPath() to ensure previous drawing commands do not interfere with the new shape.
Use moveTo(x, y) to position the virtual 'pen' at the starting vertex without drawing anything.
Use lineTo(x, y) repeatedly to define each subsequent vertex of the polygon.
Call closePath() to automatically connect the last point back to the first, then use fill() or stroke() to render the shape.

Elements.Title: Applying Fill and Stroke Styles
Elements.BodyText: The visual appearance of shapes is controlled by setting fillStyle and strokeStyle properties on the context before drawing.
Elements.Notes: Styles can be set using CSS color strings, hex codes, RGB/RGBA values, or even gradient and pattern objects for advanced effects.

Items.Text: ctx.fillStyle accepts any valid CSS color value and determines the interior color of filled shapes.
ctx.strokeStyle sets the color of the outline drawn by stroke() or strokeRect().
ctx.lineWidth controls the thickness of strokes in pixels; the default value is 1.
Style properties persist for all subsequent draw calls until they are explicitly changed, so set them before each shape when using different colors.

Elements.Title: Drawing Rounded Rectangles
Elements.BodyText: Rounded rectangles combine straight edges with arc-based corners and can be created using the roundRect() method or constructed manually with paths and arcs.
Elements.Notes: The roundRect() method was introduced as a native convenience method in more recent browser implementations. For older browser support, manually combining lineTo() and arcTo() calls remains a reliable approach.

Items.Text: The native roundRect(x, y, width, height, radii) method simplifies creating rectangles with rounded corners by accepting a corner radius value.
The arcTo(x1, y1, x2, y2, radius) method can be used inside a path to create smooth curved corners manually.
As with other path-based shapes, call fill() or stroke() after defining the rounded rectangle path to render it.

Topics.Title: Working with Paths and Lines
Topics.Summary: Students explore how to create complex custom shapes and line drawings by defining paths, using moveTo, lineTo, and related methods to construct precise graphics.

Elements.Title: Understanding the Path Concept
Elements.BodyText: A path in the HTML5 Canvas is an invisible set of connected points and curves that defines the outline of a shape before it is rendered.
Elements.Notes: Think of a path like lifting a pen and plotting a route on paper — nothing is drawn until you explicitly stroke or fill the path.

Items.Text: Paths must be explicitly started with beginPath() before any drawing commands are issued.
A single path can consist of multiple sub-paths, straight lines, arcs, and curves.
Paths are not visible on the canvas until you call stroke() or fill() to render them.

Elements.Title: Starting and Resetting a Path with beginPath()
Elements.BodyText: The beginPath() method resets the current drawing path, ensuring that new drawing commands do not connect to or interfere with previously defined paths.
Elements.Notes: Forgetting to call beginPath() is a common mistake that causes unintended lines to appear, connecting the end of one shape to the start of another.

Items.Text: Call ctx.beginPath() at the start of every new shape or line sequence to clear the previous path.
Without beginPath(), subsequent moveTo() and lineTo() calls will be appended to the existing path.
beginPath() does not visually erase anything already drawn on the canvas; it only resets the path definition.

Elements.Title: Moving the Drawing Cursor with moveTo()
Elements.BodyText: The moveTo(x, y) method repositions the starting point of a new sub-path to the specified coordinates without drawing any line.
Elements.Notes: moveTo() is analogous to lifting a pen and placing it at a new location on the page — it sets the origin for the next drawing command.

Items.Text: moveTo(x, y) accepts canvas pixel coordinates and places the 'pen' at that position.
It is typically the first command after beginPath() to set where drawing will begin.
Multiple moveTo() calls within one path create separate sub-paths, allowing disconnected shapes in a single draw call.

Elements.Title: Drawing Straight Lines with lineTo()
Elements.BodyText: The lineTo(x, y) method draws a straight line from the current position of the drawing cursor to the specified (x, y) coordinate.
Elements.Notes: Each subsequent lineTo() call continues from the endpoint of the previous line, making it straightforward to chain multiple line segments together.

Items.Text: lineTo(x, y) updates the current point to (x, y) after defining the line segment.
Chaining multiple lineTo() calls creates a polyline or multi-segment path.
The line is not rendered until stroke() or fill() is called on the path.

Elements.Title: Closing a Path with closePath()
Elements.BodyText: The closePath() method draws a straight line from the current point back to the starting point of the path, automatically closing the shape.
Elements.Notes: closePath() is especially useful when drawing polygons, as it ensures the final edge of the shape is cleanly connected without gaps at the corner join.

Items.Text: closePath() is optional — open paths (like standalone lines) do not require it.
When used before fill(), it ensures the filled region is properly enclosed.
closePath() does not end the path; you can continue adding sub-paths afterward.

Elements.Title: Rendering Paths with stroke() and fill()
Elements.BodyText: Once a path is defined, stroke() renders the outline of the path and fill() fills the enclosed area with the current fill color or style.
Elements.Notes: You can call both stroke() and fill() on the same path to produce a shape with both a visible border and a colored interior.

Items.Text: stroke() uses the current strokeStyle, lineWidth, and related properties to draw the path outline.
fill() uses the current fillStyle and automatically closes any open sub-paths before filling.
The order of stroke() and fill() matters: calling fill() last draws it on top of the stroke, and vice versa.

Elements.Title: Controlling Line Appearance
Elements.BodyText: The Canvas API provides several properties to control how stroked lines look, including their width, end caps, and join style between segments.
Elements.Notes: Adjusting these properties before calling stroke() allows fine-grained control over the visual style of any path-based drawing.

Items.Text: ctx.lineWidth sets the thickness of stroked lines in pixels; the default value is 1.
ctx.lineCap controls the shape of line endpoints and can be set to 'butt', 'round', or 'square'.
ctx.lineJoin controls how two connected line segments meet, with options 'miter', 'round', and 'bevel'.
ctx.setLineDash() accepts an array of numbers to create dashed or dotted line patterns along a path.

Topics.Title: Applying Colors, Styles, and Gradients
Topics.Summary: This topic introduces fill and stroke styles, including solid colors, gradients, and patterns, enabling students to add visual richness and detail to their canvas drawings.

Elements.Title: Fill and Stroke Style Basics
Elements.BodyText: The Canvas API provides two fundamental style properties — fillStyle and strokeStyle — that control how shapes and paths are colored when drawn.
Elements.Notes: These properties accept CSS color strings, gradient objects, or pattern objects, giving developers flexible control over appearance.

Items.Text: Set ctx.fillStyle before calling fill() to apply an interior color to a closed shape.
Set ctx.strokeStyle before calling stroke() to apply a color to the outline or path of a shape.
Both properties default to black (#000000) if not explicitly set.
Changes to these properties persist and affect all subsequent drawing operations until changed again.

Elements.Title: Using Solid Colors
Elements.BodyText: Solid colors are the simplest style option and can be specified using any valid CSS color format, including named colors, hex codes, rgb(), rgba(), hsl(), and hsla().
Elements.Notes: Using rgba() or hsla() allows you to control opacity directly within the color value, which is useful for layering semi-transparent shapes.

Items.Text: Named colors (e.g., 'red', 'blue') and hex codes (e.g., '#ff0000') are the most concise way to set a solid color.
The rgb() function lets you define colors numerically using red, green, and blue channel values from 0 to 255.
The rgba() function adds an alpha channel (0.0 to 1.0) for transparency, enabling blending effects between layers.
hsl() and hsla() specify color via hue, saturation, and lightness, which can be more intuitive for programmatic color generation.

Elements.Title: Creating Linear Gradients
Elements.BodyText: A linear gradient transitions colors along a straight line and is created using the ctx.createLinearGradient() method, then assigned as a fillStyle or strokeStyle.
Elements.Notes: The gradient's start and end coordinates define the direction of the color transition — for example, left-to-right or top-to-bottom.

Items.Text: Call ctx.createLinearGradient(x0, y0, x1, y1) to define a gradient from point (x0, y0) to point (x1, y1).
Use gradient.addColorStop(offset, color) to set color values at positions from 0.0 (start) to 1.0 (end).
Multiple color stops can be added to create multi-color transitions across the gradient line.
Assign the resulting gradient object to ctx.fillStyle or ctx.strokeStyle before drawing to apply it.

Elements.Title: Creating Radial Gradients
Elements.BodyText: A radial gradient transitions colors outward from one circle to another and is created using ctx.createRadialGradient(), producing a spherical or spotlight-like color effect.
Elements.Notes: Radial gradients are useful for simulating lighting, glows, and three-dimensional shading on circular or elliptical shapes.

Items.Text: Call ctx.createRadialGradient(x0, y0, r0, x1, y1, r1) to define an inner circle and an outer circle for the gradient.
Color stops work the same as in linear gradients — offset 0.0 maps to the inner circle and 1.0 to the outer circle.
Setting different centers for the two circles creates an off-center glow or lens-flare effect.
Like linear gradients, the object is assigned to fillStyle or strokeStyle before calling fill() or stroke().

Elements.Title: Working with Patterns
Elements.BodyText: Patterns allow an image, canvas, or video element to be tiled and used as a fill or stroke style, enabling textured and repeating backgrounds within canvas drawings.
Elements.Notes: Patterns are especially useful for creating backgrounds, decorative fills, or simulating surface textures such as wood or fabric.

Items.Text: Create a pattern with ctx.createPattern(image, repetition), where repetition can be 'repeat', 'repeat-x', 'repeat-y', or 'no-repeat'.
The image source can be an HTMLImageElement, an HTMLCanvasElement, or an HTMLVideoElement.
Once created, assign the pattern object to ctx.fillStyle or ctx.strokeStyle just like a color or gradient.
The pattern tiles relative to the canvas origin, so using transforms can shift or rotate how the pattern is applied.

Elements.Title: Controlling Global Opacity and Composite Operations
Elements.BodyText: Beyond per-color transparency, the Canvas API provides globalAlpha and globalCompositeOperation properties to control how new drawing operations interact visually with existing canvas content.
Elements.Notes: Composite operations can produce creative blending effects similar to layer blend modes found in image editing software.

Items.Text: Set ctx.globalAlpha to a value between 0.0 and 1.0 to apply uniform transparency to all subsequent drawing operations.
ctx.globalCompositeOperation determines how new shapes are blended with existing pixels — common values include 'source-over', 'multiply', and 'screen'.
Unlike rgba() transparency, globalAlpha affects fills, strokes, images, and text simultaneously.
Resetting globalAlpha to 1.0 and globalCompositeOperation to 'source-over' restores default drawing behavior.

Topics.Title: Rendering Text on the Canvas
Topics.Summary: Students learn how to render and style text on the canvas, including setting font properties, alignment, and applying fill or stroke to textual content.

Elements.Title: Setting Up the Canvas Context for Text
Elements.BodyText: Before rendering text, you must obtain a 2D rendering context from the canvas element using JavaScript.
Elements.Notes: Use `const ctx = canvas.getContext('2d');` to access the context on which all text drawing methods are called.

Items.Text: The 2D context object (`ctx`) exposes all text-related properties and methods.
Text rendering shares the same context used for shapes and paths, so property changes affect all subsequent drawing operations.
Always reference the canvas element via `document.getElementById()` or a similar selector before calling `getContext('2d')`.

Elements.Title: Configuring the Font Property
Elements.BodyText: The `font` property of the 2D context defines the typeface, size, and style applied to rendered text, using CSS font syntax.
Elements.Notes: Example: `ctx.font = 'bold 24px Arial';` sets bold text at 24 pixels in the Arial typeface. If not set, the default is typically `'10px sans-serif'`.

Items.Text: The value assigned to `ctx.font` follows the same shorthand syntax as the CSS `font` property (e.g., `'italic 16px Georgia'`).
Font size must include a unit such as `px`, `em`, or `pt`; omitting units may cause the property to be ignored.
Multiple style tokens like `bold`, `italic`, and `small-caps` can be combined before the size and family (e.g., `'italic bold 20px Verdana'`).

Elements.Title: Filling Text with fillText()
Elements.BodyText: The `fillText()` method renders solid, filled text at a specified position on the canvas using the current `fillStyle`.
Elements.Notes: Syntax: `ctx.fillText(text, x, y [, maxWidth]);`. The optional `maxWidth` parameter constrains the rendered width, compressing the text if necessary.

Items.Text: Set `ctx.fillStyle` to a color, gradient, or pattern before calling `fillText()` to control the text's fill appearance.
The `x` and `y` coordinates define the text's anchor point, which is influenced by the current alignment and baseline settings.
Passing a `maxWidth` value causes the browser to scale or condense the text so it does not exceed that pixel width.

Elements.Title: Stroking Text with strokeText()
Elements.BodyText: The `strokeText()` method renders text as an outline only, using the current `strokeStyle` and `lineWidth` settings.
Elements.Notes: Example: `ctx.strokeStyle = 'navy'; ctx.lineWidth = 2; ctx.strokeText('Hello', 50, 100);` produces navy-outlined text with no fill.

Items.Text: Unlike `fillText()`, `strokeText()` draws only the border of each character, leaving the interior transparent.
`strokeStyle` accepts the same values as `fillStyle`: named colors, hex codes, RGB, or gradients.
`fillText()` and `strokeText()` can be combined on the same text string to produce filled text with a visible outline.

Elements.Title: Controlling Text Alignment
Elements.BodyText: The `textAlign` property determines the horizontal positioning of text relative to the x-coordinate passed to `fillText()` or `strokeText()`.
Elements.Notes: Possible values are `'left'`, `'right'`, `'center'`, `'start'`, and `'end'`. The default is `'start'`, which behaves like `'left'` in left-to-right locales.

Items.Text: Setting `ctx.textAlign = 'center'` causes the text to be centered horizontally around the given x position, useful for labels on charts or centered headings.
`'left'` and `'right'` anchor the text so its left or right edge falls at the x coordinate, respectively.
`'start'` and `'end'` respect the writing direction of the document, making them preferable for internationalized applications.

Elements.Title: Setting the Text Baseline
Elements.BodyText: The `textBaseline` property controls vertical alignment by defining which part of the text's line box aligns with the y-coordinate.
Elements.Notes: Common values include `'alphabetic'` (default), `'top'`, `'middle'`, `'bottom'`, `'hanging'`, and `'ideographic'`.

Items.Text: The default `'alphabetic'` baseline aligns text as it would appear in standard Latin script typography.
Using `'middle'` centers the text vertically around the y coordinate, which is helpful when positioning labels relative to shapes.
Choosing the right baseline is important for precise layout, especially when mixing text with drawn graphics or icons.

Elements.Title: Measuring Text Width with measureText()
Elements.BodyText: The `measureText()` method returns a `TextMetrics` object that provides the measured width of a string given the current font settings.
Elements.Notes: Example: `const metrics = ctx.measureText('Hello World'); const width = metrics.width;`. This is particularly useful for dynamically centering or wrapping text.

Items.Text: Call `ctx.measureText(string)` before drawing to calculate the pixel width a string will occupy on the canvas.
The returned `TextMetrics` object's `width` property reflects the current `ctx.font` setting, so always set the font first.
Text wrapping logic can be built manually by measuring words and accumulating widths until a line exceeds the available canvas width.

Topics.Title: Drawing and Manipulating Images
Topics.Summary: This topic covers how to load and draw images onto the canvas using the drawImage method, including techniques for cropping and scaling image content.

Elements.Title: Loading an Image for Canvas Use
Elements.BodyText: Before drawing an image onto the canvas, it must be loaded into the browser using a JavaScript Image object.
Elements.Notes: It is critical to wait for the image to fully load before attempting to draw it, otherwise the canvas will render a blank or broken result.

Items.Text: Create an Image object in JavaScript using `const img = new Image();` and set its `src` property to the image file path.
Attach an `onload` event handler to the image object to ensure drawing code only executes after the image has fully loaded.
Attempting to draw an image before it loads is a common mistake that results in nothing appearing on the canvas.

Elements.Title: Basic drawImage Usage
Elements.BodyText: The `drawImage` method is the primary Canvas API function used to render an image onto the canvas at a specified position.
Elements.Notes: The simplest form of drawImage takes three arguments: the image source, and the x and y coordinates where the image's top-left corner will be placed.

Items.Text: Call `ctx.drawImage(img, x, y)` to draw the image at coordinates (x, y) using the image's natural width and height.
The `ctx` variable refers to the 2D rendering context obtained from the canvas element via `canvas.getContext('2d')`.
The image is drawn relative to the canvas coordinate system, with (0, 0) at the top-left corner of the canvas.

Elements.Title: Scaling Images with drawImage
Elements.BodyText: The `drawImage` method supports an extended signature that allows you to specify a destination width and height, enabling image scaling.
Elements.Notes: Scaling can enlarge or shrink an image to fit a particular area of the canvas, which is useful for thumbnails, responsive layouts, or animations.

Items.Text: Use `ctx.drawImage(img, x, y, width, height)` to draw the image scaled to the specified width and height dimensions.
Setting width and height values smaller than the original shrinks the image, while larger values stretch it.
Aspect ratio is not automatically preserved; you must calculate proportional dimensions manually if needed.

Elements.Title: Cropping Images with drawImage
Elements.BodyText: The full nine-argument form of `drawImage` enables cropping by defining a source rectangle within the original image before drawing it to the canvas.
Elements.Notes: This technique is widely used in sprite sheet animation, where different sections of a single image file represent individual frames or icons.

Items.Text: The full syntax is `ctx.drawImage(img, sx, sy, sWidth, sHeight, dx, dy, dWidth, dHeight)`, where `s` parameters define the source crop region and `d` parameters define the destination on the canvas.
`sx` and `sy` specify the top-left corner of the crop region within the source image, measured in pixels.
`sWidth` and `sHeight` define how wide and tall the cropped region is within the source image.
The cropped region is then drawn and optionally scaled to fit the destination rectangle defined by `dx`, `dy`, `dWidth`, and `dHeight`.

Elements.Title: Combining Cropping and Scaling
Elements.BodyText: Using all nine arguments of `drawImage` simultaneously allows you to both crop a specific region of an image and scale that region to a different size on the canvas.
Elements.Notes: For example, a sprite sheet might contain a 32x32 pixel sprite that you crop and then scale up to 128x128 pixels for display.

Items.Text: Specify a small source crop region and a larger destination region to magnify a portion of the image on the canvas.
Specify a large source region and a smaller destination region to create a miniaturized view of part of the image.
This combination is essential for sprite-based animations and image gallery thumbnail generation.

Elements.Title: Practical Considerations for Image Drawing
Elements.BodyText: Working with images on the canvas involves several practical concerns related to performance, cross-origin access, and canvas state.
Elements.Notes: Being aware of these considerations helps avoid common errors and ensures images render correctly across different browsers and environments.

Items.Text: Images loaded from a different domain may trigger CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) restrictions, preventing them from being drawn onto the canvas.
To avoid CORS issues with external images, the server must include appropriate `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers, or the image must be served from the same origin.
Redrawing images repeatedly in an animation loop is common; clearing the canvas with `ctx.clearRect()` before each frame prevents image ghosting.
Caching the Image object rather than creating a new one each frame significantly improves rendering performance.

Topics.Title: Foundations of Interactive Canvas Graphics
Topics.Summary: Students are introduced to the principles of combining Canvas drawing with JavaScript events to create dynamic and interactive browser-based graphics experiences.

Elements.Title: The Canvas Element as an Interactive Surface
Elements.BodyText: The HTML5 Canvas element serves as a programmable drawing surface that can respond to user input, making it the foundation for interactive browser-based graphics.
Elements.Notes: Unlike static images, a canvas can be redrawn at any time in response to events, enabling experiences like drawing apps, games, and data visualizations.

Items.Text: The <canvas> element is placed in HTML and accessed via JavaScript using getElementById or querySelector.
A 2D rendering context is obtained with getContext('2d'), providing all drawing methods and properties.
The canvas coordinate system starts at (0, 0) in the top-left corner, with x increasing right and y increasing downward.
Canvas dimensions are set with the width and height attributes directly on the element to avoid scaling distortion.

Elements.Title: Connecting JavaScript Events to Canvas
Elements.BodyText: Interactivity is achieved by attaching JavaScript event listeners to the canvas element, allowing user actions such as mouse clicks and movements to trigger drawing operations.
Elements.Notes: Common events used with canvas include mousemove, mousedown, mouseup, click, touchstart, and touchmove for mobile support.

Items.Text: Event listeners are added using addEventListener(), specifying the event type and a callback function to execute.
The callback function receives an event object containing details such as mouse position and button state.
Multiple event listeners can be combined to handle complex interactions like click-and-drag drawing.
Removing event listeners with removeEventListener() is important for managing memory and preventing unintended behavior.

Elements.Title: Translating Mouse Coordinates to Canvas Space
Elements.BodyText: Mouse event coordinates are reported relative to the browser viewport, so they must be converted to canvas-relative coordinates before drawing accurately.
Elements.Notes: Using getBoundingClientRect() is the most reliable method for this conversion, especially when the canvas is offset from the page origin or scaled via CSS.

Items.Text: The event properties clientX and clientY give the mouse position relative to the viewport.
Subtracting the canvas element's bounding rectangle's left and top values converts viewport coordinates to canvas coordinates.
CSS scaling or padding on the canvas element can introduce additional offsets that must be accounted for.
Storing the computed canvas-relative coordinates in variables makes them reusable across multiple drawing calls.

Elements.Title: Redrawing the Canvas for Dynamic Graphics
Elements.BodyText: Because the canvas is a pixel-based surface with no retained object model, creating dynamic visuals requires clearing and redrawing the canvas in response to state changes or user input.
Elements.Notes: This clear-and-redraw pattern is central to animation loops, interactive tools, and any scenario where the visual output must update over time.

Items.Text: clearRect(0, 0, canvas.width, canvas.height) erases all pixels on the canvas before each redraw.
Application state (such as shape positions or user-drawn paths) is stored in JavaScript variables or arrays, not in the canvas itself.
On each relevant event or animation frame, the stored state is used to re-render the entire scene from scratch.
This approach keeps rendering logic predictable and separates data management from drawing concerns.

Elements.Title: Using requestAnimationFrame for Smooth Interactivity
Elements.BodyText: For continuously animated or highly responsive canvas experiences, requestAnimationFrame provides a browser-optimized loop that synchronizes redraws with the display refresh rate.
Elements.Notes: requestAnimationFrame is preferred over setInterval or setTimeout for animations because it pauses automatically when the tab is not visible, saving CPU and battery resources.

Items.Text: requestAnimationFrame accepts a callback function that is called before the next browser repaint, typically 60 times per second.
The animation loop is sustained by calling requestAnimationFrame recursively within its own callback.
Interactive input events update shared state variables, and the animation loop reads that state to render each frame.
Combining event-driven state updates with a requestAnimationFrame loop decouples input handling from rendering for smoother results.

Elements.Title: Structuring Interactive Canvas Code
Elements.BodyText: Well-organized code separates concerns into initialization, event handling, state management, and rendering, making interactive canvas programs easier to build, read, and maintain.
Elements.Notes: As projects grow, patterns such as separating a draw() function, an update() function, and an init() function help manage complexity and support future enhancements.

Items.Text: An init() function sets up the canvas, context, initial state, and event listeners when the page loads.
Event handler functions update application state variables but do not perform drawing directly.
A dedicated draw() or render() function reads current state and issues all canvas drawing commands.
Keeping drawing logic in one place makes it straightforward to add features like undo history or multi-layer graphics.
